channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Abortion Pill Lawsuit Leaves Trump in a Political Bind Ahead of the Midterms
item.description: Louisiana wants the Food and Drug Administration to curtail access to the medication. Doing so could cost Republicans at the polls.
item.dc:creator: Pam Belluck and Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:description: A lawsuit against the F.D.A. threatens access to medication abortion, and the Trump administration has remained strikingly silent about the case, even as it reached the Supreme Court.

item.title: Trump’s Approval Sinks Amid Unpopular War, Darkening G.O.P. Prospects
item.description: With the midterms nearing, President Trump’s approval rating has hit a second-term low as voters question his handling of the economy, according to the latest New York Times/Siena poll.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer, Ruth Igielnik and Camille Baker
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Elon Musk Loses His Blockbuster Case Against OpenAI
item.description: Also, a railroad strike disrupts commutes into New York City. Here’s the latest at the end of Monday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times

item.title: Trump Says He Authorized New Strikes on Iran, but Has Decided to Hold Off
item.description: President Trump has repeatedly threatened new military action against Iran, only to pull back from resuming major fighting in a deeply unpopular, expensive conflict.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A U.S. military official said that Iran had been able to study the flight patterns of American fighter jets and bombers.

item.title: L-I-R-R vs. Lurr: The Debate Splitting New York Commuters
item.description: Initialism or acronym? It doesn’t matter. No one can agree on how to pronounce it.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Keh
item.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Long Island Rail Road is frequently abbreviated to L.I.R.R. But how is that pronounced?

item.title: L.I.R.R. Strike Could Play a Role in New York Governor’s Race
item.description: Gov. Kathy Hochul, a Democrat, is running for a second full term against the Nassau County executive, Bruce Blakeman, a Republican who has criticized her response to the labor dispute.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes

item.title: Tonys 2026 Predictions: Who Will Win? And Who Should?
item.description: Our chief theater critic looks at this year’s nominees and makes some predictions (and recommendations).
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) (Play)
item.media:description: Susannah Flood, seated, and fellow cast members in “Liberation.”

item.title: Elon Musk Loses $150 Billion Suit Against OpenAI and Sam Altman
item.description: A nine-member jury found that Mr. Musk had waited too long to sue. The setback for the tech mogul frees OpenAI to continue in the artificial intelligence race.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: OpenAI’s president, Greg Brockman, right, was also named in Mr. Musk’s lawsuit.

item.title: After Elon Musk’s Court Loss Comes the Long Hot A.I. Summer
item.description: Even as protests increase, the collapse of Mr. Musk’s suit against OpenAI and Sam Altman will speed up the artificial intelligence juggernaut.
item.dc:creator: David Streitfeld and Natallie Rocha
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Elon Musk on Monday lost his lawsuit accusing OpenAI and its chief executive, Sam Altman, of breaching the company’s founding contract.

item.title: Five Takeaways From the Blockbuster Trial Pitting Elon Musk Against OpenAI
item.description: It took a jury less than two hours to decide that Mr. Musk had waited too long to sue. But the testimony over three weeks was still illuminating.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: The federal courthouse in Oakland, Calif., on Monday, when a jury decided that Elon Musk had waited too long to file his lawsuit against OpenAI.

item.title: Meta Reassigns 7,000 Employees to Focus on A.I.
item.description: The company announced the changes two days before it plans to lay off 10 percent of its work force, or about 8,000 employees.
item.dc:creator: Eli Tan
item.media:credit: Adrienne Grunwald for The New York Times
item.media:description: The new Meta Lab flagship store, which sells tech like smart glasses and virtual reality headsets, on Fifth Avenue in Manhattan.

item.title: What is Trump’s $1.776 Billion ‘Anti-Weaponization’ Fund?
item.description: The Trump administration is creating a $1.8 billion fund to compensate people it says were wronged by the federal government, a group that could be largely made up of the president’s allies.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department is tapping an unlimited fund created by Congress to settle lawsuits filed against the government.

item.title: ICE Agent Charged in Shooting of a Venezuelan Immigrant in Minnesota
item.description: The agent is the second federal officer to face felony charges in Minnesota stemming from Operation Metro Surge, the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño and Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police blocked off an area in Minneapolis near the shooting on Jan. 14.

item.title: Federal Prosecutors Move to Drop Bribery Case Against Indian Magnate
item.description: Gautam Adani had been accused of lying to American investors about payoffs in India. The end of the case came as President Trump has signaled a transactional approach to justice.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar and Alex Travelli
item.media:description: Gautam Adani’s companies have powered India’s rise as a global economic power.

item.title: Sandy Fire in Simi Valley Forces Immediate Evacuations
item.description: Officials ordered more than 23,000 to evacuate after a brush fire broke out in Simi Valley, Calif.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:description: The blaze, called the Sandy fire, broke out on a warm, breezy day in Simi Valley.

item.title: L.A. County Response to Altadena Fire Was Not Biased, Report Finds
item.description: An examination of the disaster commissioned by the county concluded that its efforts were muddled and chaotic, but said that it had not discriminated against Black residents.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nearly all of the 19 people who died in the Eaton fire lived in West Altadena.

item.title: Channel Islands Fire Started Accidentally by Shipwrecked Sailor
item.description: The shipwrecked man was rescued from Channel Islands National Park, but the flares set off a 10,000-acre fire that has burned park buildings, forced evacuations and threatened rare Torrey pines.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla

item.title: He’s Been Impeached and Indicted. He Has a Chance in Texas’s Senate Race.
item.description: The fate of Ken Paxton, the conservative Texas firebrand, could decide whether Republicans keep control of the Senate.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kruse and Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times

item.title: Ebola Was Identified in Congo Weeks Before W.H.O. Declared an Emergency
item.description: Early surveillance and testing failed to identify the rare species of Ebola responsible for the current outbreak. An American doctor is among the confirmed cases.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: At a hospital in Ituri Province, Democratic Republic of Congo, the center of a new Ebola outbreak.

item.title: American Man Tests Positive for Ebola Amid Outbreak, Officials Say
item.description: An American doctor working with a Christian missionary organization in the Democratic Republic of Congo was exposed to the deadly virus while treating patients, the organization said.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang and Hannah Ziegler
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: People wait near an ambulance at a hospital in Bunia, Congo on Sunday.

item.title: Citing Ebola Outbreak, U.S. Restricts Entry From Congo, Uganda and South Sudan
item.description: The order is in effect for 30 days and does not apply to American citizens or U.S. service members.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Maggie Haberman
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: A Customs and Border Protection officer at John F. Kennedy International Airport in New York.

item.title: What to Know About the Ebola Outbreak, as U.S. Restricts Travelers
item.description: The United States restricted entry of people who have been to three African countries, after the World Health Organization declared a global health emergency. The outbreak has killed more than 100.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: A motorcycle taxi driver at a hospital in Goma, Democratic Republic of Congo, on Sunday.

item.title: Mark Fuhrman, Flawed Witness in O.J. Simpson Trial, Dies at 74
item.description: A Los Angeles police detective, he was discredited during Mr. Simpson’s 1995 murder trial by defense lawyers who pointed to his past use of racist language.
item.dc:creator: Clyde Haberman
item.media:description: Mark Fuhrman on the witness stand during the 1995 murder trial of O.J. Simpson. He was called as a star witness for the prosecution but was thoroughly discredited by Mr. Simpson’s lawyers.

item.title: Gun Found in Mangione’s Backpack Can be Used as Evidence, Judge Says
item.description: The ruling was a partial victory for prosecutors in the murder trial of Luigi Mangione. A state judge ruled that some other material and statements could not be used.
item.dc:creator: Hurubie Meko and Anusha Bayya
item.media:description: Luigi Mangione’s lawyers have argued that the evidence found in his backpack when he was arrested was obtained illegally and cannot be used at trial.

item.title: Actually, Democracy Dies in H.R.
item.description: New research sheds light on how mediocre employees help would-be authoritarians maintain power.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Taub
item.category: Making a Career in Dictatorship: The Secret Logic behind Repression and Coups (Book)
item.media:description: Argentine security forces seizing a young man in 1982 in Buenos Aires during the Dirty War.

item.title: Firing Squads Expose the Brutality of the Death Penalty
item.description: America is returning to firing squad executions after failing to find a more humane way to carry out the death penalty.
item.dc:creator: Maurice Chammah

item.title: South Korea and Japan’s Leaders Are Set to Meet: What to Know
item.description: Japan’s prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, and South Korea’s president, Lee Jae Myung, have surprised many observers by moving past their nations’ historical grievances.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:description: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi of Japan and President Lee Jae Myung of South Korea at the Horyu-ji temple in Japan in January.

item.title: American Hantavirus Patient Ordered to Stay in Quarantine Despite Plans to Leave
item.description: An American from the hantavirus-infected cruise ship, who said she had tested negative for the disease, has been ordered to remain in an Omaha quarantine facility for two more weeks.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: The Davis Global Center, at the University of Nebraska Medical Center campus in Omaha, houses the National Quarantine Unit.

item.title: New York Times Sues Pentagon for a Second Time
item.description: The Times is challenging a new requirement that reporters covering the military complex have an official escort, part of a broader legal challenge to the Pentagon’s press restrictions.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The lawsuit is the second one from The New York Times over the Defense Department’s restrictions on the press.

item.title: Étienne Davignon Dies Before Trial in Patrice Lumumba Killing
item.description: Étienne Davignon had been set to stand trial in connection with the murder of Patrice Lumumba, the first prime minister of what is now the Democratic Republic of Congo.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Benoit Doppagne/BELGA, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Étienne Davignon in 2018.
